Quote: הֲ⁠לוֹא אֵ֣לֶּה כֻלָּ֗⁠ם עָלָי⁠ו֙ מָשָׁ֣ל יִשָּׂ֔אוּ וּ⁠מְלִיצָ֖ה חִיד֣וֹת ל֑⁠וֹ וְ⁠יֹאמַ֗ר ה֚וֹי הַ⁠מַּרְבֶּ֣ה לֹּא ל֔⁠וֹ עַד מָתַ֕י וּ⁠מַכְבִּ֥יד עָלָ֖י⁠ו עַבְטִֽיט
Note: Yahweh is using the question form to emphasize that the conquered peoples will certainly mock the oppressor. If a speaker of your language would not use the question form for that purpose, you could translate this as a statement or as an exclamation. Alternate translation: [These, all of them, will certainly take up a proverb against him, and ridicule, a riddle about him, so that it says, ‘Woe to the one increasing what is not his —until when? and loading onto himself pledges!]

Quote: וְ⁠יֹאמַ֗ר ה֚וֹי הַ⁠מַּרְבֶּ֣ה לֹּא ל֔⁠וֹ עַד מָתַ֕י וּ⁠מַכְבִּ֥יד עָלָ֖י⁠ו עַבְטִֽיט
Note: If it would be clearer in your language, you could translate this so that there is not a quotation within a quotation. Alternate translation: [so that it pronounces woe on the one increasing what is not his —until when? and loading onto himself pledges]

Quote: ה֚וֹי הַ⁠מַּרְבֶּ֣ה לֹּא ל֔⁠וֹ עַד מָתַ֕י וּ⁠מַכְבִּ֥יד עָלָ֖י⁠ו עַבְטִֽיט
Note: Here the conquered peoples use a proverb in order to mock the greedy conqueror. Translate this proverb in a way that will be recognized as a proverb and be meaningful in your language and culture. Alternate translation: [Disaster awaits the one who endlessly takes what does not belong to him and burdens himself with debts]

The conquered peoples are using the question form to express frustration that the oppressor’s greed has continued for too long. If a speaker of your language would not use the question form for that purpose, you could translate this as a statement or as an exclamation. Alternate translation: [for far too long]
